# Brief: Marcus (PERSONA-004)
## Upcoming Meeting: 2026-04-07

## Since Last Meeting

No new evidence has been provided in the gap between April 2 and tonight's meeting. That is a normal four-day span between a Thursday school board session and a Tuesday council meeting. The last documented facts remain: the board unanimously deferred the budget on April 2; $300,000 in additional state funding was announced but unconfirmed; director-level restructuring savings were confirmed on the record at $20,000–$30,000; and the union formally identified several dozen meet-and-consult and bargaining obligations generated by reconfiguration. Tonight's public hearing is the first public event in the budget calendar since that deferral.

---

## Open Questions

Carrying into tonight:

- **Will the school budget presentation actually show FTE counts?** Every time this has come to council it's been dollar figures. The staffing story — 32 teachers on a recall list, 6 FTE gone from SPHS — needs to be visible to the people voting on it, not buried in line items. Will tonight be different?
- **Has the $300,000 state funding been confirmed?** And if it has, does anyone have a defined, equitable process for deciding which positions it restores — or are we heading into a lobbying-volume free-for-all that Anderson already warned against?
- **Will the structural problem get named?** Ketchem confirmed on the record that labor costs outpace the 6% ceiling every year. Does anyone present that to the council tonight, or does this get framed as a one-time fix?
- **What is the council's actual posture going into April 14?** We know school is the first item at Budget Workshop #1. Does tonight's hearing set up a concrete revenue conversation, or does it produce another deferral dressed as deliberation?
- **Are the meet-and-consult obligations costed into the budget being presented tonight?** The union identified dozens of obligations generated by reconfiguration. If no cost estimate exists, that's a hole in the document the council is being asked to evaluate.
- **Is there a coordinated union plan for tonight's testimony?** Teachers have been texting councilors directly. Is that being channeled into organized, sequenced public comment — or is it going to be individual voices without a clear ask?
- **Will anyone on the council name the recall list as a concrete problem?** Scott connected housing stability to staffing. Matthews acknowledged educator outreach. Does that translate into a specific ask tonight, or does it stay at the level of general concern?
- **If the $20,000–$30,000 administrative savings figure comes up, will anyone challenge it on the record?** That number was confirmed April 2. It needs to be contrasted publicly with what a single teaching position costs.
- **What happens to the SPHS content losses in the public narrative?** Two science positions, one English, one World Language/ESOL, two special ed case managers, one CTE, one social work — these are named losses for specific students. Do they appear in tonight's presentation or get absorbed into aggregate numbers?

---

## Agenda Implications

### Section D — Board and Committee Vacancies

Sixteen board and committee appointments are listed, including current vacancies (two created in January) and three anticipated vacancies from terms ending May 4. The May 4 expiration is nine days before Budget Workshop #3 and three weeks before the May 5 council vote on sending the school budget to referendum.

**What this means for Marcus:** Governance continuity on appointed bodies matters less to him than the elected council vote. But the volume of vacancies is a signal about municipal bandwidth. A council managing sixteen appointments alongside a contested school budget presentation has less floor time for fiscal depth.

**What to listen for:** Whether any vacancy affects a committee that has been involved in school budget or development decisions — particularly any TIF or economic development oversight bodies.

**Prepare:** No specific question, but note which vacancies are filled tonight versus tabled.

---

### Section E — Consent Agenda Orders (E.1–E.11, including Speed Grant, Community Affairs Grant, Pool Deck Bid, SMCC Trail Agreement)

These are administrative and housekeeping items: routine orders, grant acceptances, a bid award for pool deck flooring at SPCC, and a trail/beach patrol agreement with SMCC. None directly affect school staffing.

**What this means for Marcus:** Each grant acceptance and expenditure commitment is a legitimate municipal function. The pool deck flooring alone ($40,000+) is worth noting only for framing — not to argue against infrastructure, but because Marcus will be asked to justify restoring teaching positions in a meeting that also approves routine city spending without debate. That framing contrast is real and is worth being prepared for.

**What to listen for:** Whether any consent item draws objection or discussion that signals council fiscal anxiety — which could foreshadow the posture they bring to G.2.

**Prepare:** No challenge to consent items is warranted or productive. Know the numbers so you're not caught off-guard if someone cites them comparatively.

---

### Section G.1 — Tres Leches Liquor License

A routine liquor license hearing. Not relevant to Marcus.

---

### Section G.2 — Budget Presentation and Public Hearing *(primary focus)*

This is the meeting. The city manager's position paper includes the full budget calendar — April 7 presentation and hearing, April 14 Workshop #1 (School listed first), May 5 school budget approval vote, June 9 referendum. Tonight is simultaneously a presentation *to* the council and a public hearing where community members can testify.

**What this means for Marcus:** This is the last scheduled public comment opportunity before the April 14 workshop where school staffing decisions will be actively shaped. The union's organized presence tonight directly conditions how councilors walk into that workshop. If testimony tonight is fragmented and individual, councilors have no coordinated educator ask to respond to. If it's organized — sequenced, specific, tied to the recall list and program losses — it creates a defined ask that can be tracked at April 14.

**What to listen for:**
- How the school portion of the budget is presented: does it include FTE counts, or only dollar aggregates?
- Whether the $300,000 state funding figure is confirmed, quantified, or mentioned at all
- Whether the structural FY28 problem — labor costs permanently outpacing the 6% ceiling — is disclosed to the council or omitted
- Whether the $20,000–$30,000 administrative restructuring savings figure appears in the presentation or is buried
- Whether the meet-and-consult obligation costs are included in the budget document being presented
- Which councilors ask follow-up questions about staffing specifically (versus tax rate, fund balance, or aggregate cost)
- Whether any councilor asks for the FTE breakdown by building or subject area

**Prepare these questions for public comment or to prompt a colleague to raise:**
1. *The budget document presented tonight: does it show, by school and by subject area, the specific teaching positions eliminated? If not, when will the council see that breakdown before the May 5 vote?*
2. *The $20,000–$30,000 in administrative restructuring savings has been confirmed on the public record. A single teaching position costs roughly $80,000–$100,000 in salary and benefits. Can the council confirm it understands this ratio before it votes?*
3. *The Finance Director confirmed that labor costs outpace the 6% ceiling annually. Is the council receiving a projection that shows what FY28 looks like for recalled positions and staffing levels — or is the presented budget treating this as a one-time correction?*
4. *The union has identified several dozen meet-and-consult and bargaining obligations generated by the reconfiguration decision. Has the administration costed those obligations into the budget being presented tonight?*

### Section H.1 — Postponed Item (from March 3 and March 19)

This item has been delayed twice — first from March 3, then from March 19. Both of those meeting dates fall squarely within the most acute phase of the budget crisis. The position paper references multiple versions of Order #135 and a new alternative order, suggesting it is substantively contested.

**What this means for Marcus:** Without knowing the item's content, the significance is procedural: this council has been managing a contested item alongside the budget crisis for more than a month and has deferred it twice. That pattern either reflects careful deliberation or a council stretched across competing priorities — both of which matter to Marcus's assessment of how much bandwidth councilors have brought to the school budget.

**What to listen for:** The substance of the item when it's introduced. If it involves development, revenue, or land use, it may be relevant to the tax base conversation Marcus cares about.

**Prepare:** Hold judgment until the content is described from the dais.

**What to note:** The closure cost and financial assurance framing is a small signal that the council is capable of thinking in long-term liability terms when it concerns non-school infrastructure. That framing exists alongside a school budget that has no fund balance and no reserve.

## Watch For

- **Whether the school budget presentation includes FTE-level detail.** If the administration presents dollar aggregates without naming the 40 SPTA positions or the 6 SPHS FTE losses by subject area, that framing gap should be challenged in public comment. Demand specificity — "how many teachers" is a question the council should be able to answer before May 5.

- **The $300,000 state funding status.** If it is confirmed tonight, watch whether any councilor immediately ties it to position restoration — and whether they propose a process or just an outcome. Anderson's warning about position-by-position lobbying driving restoration is the right frame. Listen for whether the union has a criteria-based ask ready.

- **Councilor Matthews's response.** He confirmed educator outreach filled his phone. Tonight is the first public forum since then. His questions from the dais will reveal whether that outreach translated into a protective posture or was absorbed into general fiscal-caution framing.

- **Councilor Scott's framing.** She has connected housing stability to enrollment to staffing. Watch whether that argument appears in her questions tonight, and whether it is connected to specific position restoration or remains rhetorical.

- **Whether any councilor asks about FY28.** The structural trap is confirmed: same gap next year regardless of current cuts. If no councilor asks about FY28 at a public budget hearing, that is important information about the depth of their engagement with the finance director's testimony.

- **Who shows up to testify and in what order.** If union-coordinated testimony is organized, it will be sequenced — contract language, specific position losses, dollar comparisons, a clear ask. If it is fragmented, individual appeals without a unifying demand, the council has no concrete commitment to respond to by April 14.

- **The administrative savings figure.** If anyone cites director-level restructuring as evidence of shared sacrifice tonight, the $20,000–$30,000 confirmed figure is on the public record. That number needs to be heard alongside the cost of a single teaching position. If no one raises it from the public, Marcus or a colleague should.

- **Whether meet-and-consult obligations appear in the presentation.** The union identified dozens of obligations generated by reconfiguration. If the budget document presented tonight contains no estimate of those labor costs, that is a real gap in the document the council is evaluating. Note whether anyone raises it from the dais or in public comment.

- **The April 14 workshop setup.** School is the first item on Workshop #1. Whatever questions get asked tonight — and whatever goes unanswered — defines the homework list for April 14. Listen for councilors who explicitly say they want a specific breakdown, a confirmed number, or a staffing impact analysis before the workshop. Those stated expectations are leverage.

- **The tone of the city manager's budget presentation on schools.** The 82-positions/300-enrollment framing embedded in the council's earlier goal-setting briefing is one possible narrative. A more accurate framing would center state underfunding and documented service-driven hiring. Which narrative leads tonight's presentation sets the table for every conversation through June 9.
